title: A Multi-Country Analysis of AMR System Drivers in Africa
keyTakeaway: Ministers should institutionalise One Health governance in law, protect domestic AMR financing, and build surveillance that converts data into decisions.
executiveSummary: A political–economic analysis of AMR systems across ten African countries argues national performance depends less on laboratory hardware than on governance authority, financing and enforcement. The WHO African region carries the world's highest AMR death rate. Closing the gap between well-drafted national plans and actual delivery requires structural reform.
keyMessages: Strategic insights for ministers on governance, financing and system resilience
writeupSummary: A political–economic analysis of AMR systems across ten African countries argues national performance depends less on laboratory hardware than on governance authority, financing and enforcement. The WHO African region carries the world's highest AMR death rate. Closing the gap between well-drafted national plans and actual delivery requires structural reform.
callToAction: Ministers should institutionalise One Health governance in law, protect domestic AMR financing, and build surveillance that converts data into decisions.
alignment: Action & accountability — governance and financing reform to deliver NAPs (core 'global-to-local' theme)

title: Why Global AMR Guidance Stalls in African Systems
keyTakeaway: Ministers should fully fund and operationalise a focused set of high-leverage standards, closing the adaptation gap where AMR is actually fought.
executiveSummary: Global AMR guidance is abundant and technically sound, but a persistent gap separates what international frameworks prescribe from what national systems deliver. The brief diagnoses seven systemic barriers—governance fragmentation, thin capacity, weak laboratories, disconnected data, underfunding, poor enforcement, and failure to reach subnational levels—and shows how to close the adaptation gap.
keyMessages: What ministers need to know to convert international standards into functioning national systems
writeupSummary: Global AMR guidance is abundant and technically sound, but a persistent gap separates what international frameworks prescribe from what national systems deliver. The brief diagnoses seven systemic barriers—governance fragmentation, thin capacity, weak laboratories, disconnected data, underfunding, poor enforcement, and failure to reach subnational levels—and shows how to close the adaptation gap.
callToAction: Ministers should fully fund and operationalise a focused set of high-leverage standards, closing the adaptation gap where AMR is actually fought.
alignment: Action — translating global guidance into national systems (the conference's central 'local action' theme)

title: The Political Economy of AMR in Africa
keyTakeaway: Ministers should commit to cabinet-level accountability, protected domestic financing, and incentive-aligned regulation to govern AMR as a political-economy challenge.
executiveSummary: AMR is reframed not as a biomedical emergency but as a political-economy problem in which power, money and incentives determine outcomes. Four structural drivers dominate: misaligned incentives, donor-dependent fragility, weak regulatory enforcement, and fragmented governance. This explains why technically excellent national action plans repeatedly fail to change behaviour on the ground.
keyMessages: How power, financing and institutional interests shape — and can unblock — the AMR response
writeupSummary: AMR is reframed not as a biomedical emergency but as a political-economy problem in which power, money and incentives determine outcomes. Four structural drivers dominate: misaligned incentives, donor-dependent fragility, weak regulatory enforcement, and fragmented governance. This explains why technically excellent national action plans repeatedly fail to change behaviour on the ground.
callToAction: Ministers should commit to cabinet-level accountability, protected domestic financing, and incentive-aligned regulation to govern AMR as a political-economy challenge.
alignment: Action & accountability — political-economy reform of NAP delivery

title: Building Resilient AMR Systems Beyond Donor Dependence
keyTakeaway: Ministers should domestically finance a protected core of AMR functions, integrate donor-built systems nationally, and pool resources regionally against future shocks.
executiveSummary: A rapid 2025 contraction in US global-health assistance—a foreign-aid pause, USAID's dismantling, PEPFAR cuts—shocked African health systems, exposing how heavily AMR surveillance, laboratories and One Health coordination depended on time-limited grants. The brief examines the implications and treats the shock as both warning and opportunity to build sovereign systems.
keyMessages: Protecting surveillance, stewardship and One Health from the 2025 contraction in external assistance
writeupSummary: A rapid 2025 contraction in US global-health assistance—a foreign-aid pause, USAID's dismantling, PEPFAR cuts—shocked African health systems, exposing how heavily AMR surveillance, laboratories and One Health coordination depended on time-limited grants. The brief examines the implications and treats the shock as both warning and opportunity to build sovereign systems.
callToAction: Ministers should domestically finance a protected core of AMR functions, integrate donor-built systems nationally, and pool resources regionally against future shocks.
alignment: Sustainability — sustainable, domestic AMR financing (named conference focus)

title: Why AMR Belongs on Africa’s National Security Agenda
keyTakeaway: Heads of state should elevate AMR within national-security and development agendas, close the surveillance gap, and sustain financing as strategic investment.
executiveSummary: AMR is conventionally treated as a health-sector issue, but the brief argues it is also a national and health-security threat eroding the state's ability to protect citizens, armed forces, food systems and economy. Untreatable infections affect military readiness and stability; the African region records the world's highest AMR mortality rate.
keyMessages: Why drug resistance belongs on the agenda of heads of state and national security councils
writeupSummary: AMR is conventionally treated as a health-sector issue, but the brief argues it is also a national and health-security threat eroding the state's ability to protect citizens, armed forces, food systems and economy. Untreatable infections affect military readiness and stability; the African region records the world's highest AMR mortality rate.
callToAction: Heads of state should elevate AMR within national-security and development agendas, close the surveillance gap, and sustain financing as strategic investment.
alignment: Action — elevating AMR as a cross-government, head-of-state priority

title: Financing AMR — Domestic Budgets, Donor Leverage and Sustainability
keyTakeaway: Finance and health ministers should cost national action plans, establish protected domestic budget lines, and use donor support to build capacity.
executiveSummary: National action plans are near-universal but funding them is not: only about one in nine countries with a plan has dedicated budget, and Africa's figure is lower. This financing gap, not a shortage of technical plans, is the largest barrier to containment. The brief maps practical routes to sustainable domestic financing.
keyMessages: How ministers can move from donor-dependent plans to sustainably financed national AMR systems
writeupSummary: National action plans are near-universal but funding them is not: only about one in nine countries with a plan has dedicated budget, and Africa's figure is lower. This financing gap, not a shortage of technical plans, is the largest barrier to containment. The brief maps practical routes to sustainable domestic financing.
callToAction: Finance and health ministers should cost national action plans, establish protected domestic budget lines, and use donor support to build capacity.
alignment: Sustainability — sustainable financing mechanisms (named conference focus)

title: Strengthening One Health Governance for AMR
keyTakeaway: Ministers should institutionalise One Health governance in law, fund permanent secretariats, integrate the environment sector, and report progress to leadership.
executiveSummary: AMR is the textbook One Health problem, yet in most African countries multisectoral coordination exists on paper—committees lack legal mandate, budget authority and functioning secretariats. Regional surveys rate coordination the top priority but sustainable investment lowest. The brief draws on the Quadripartite Joint Plan to show how to make governance real.
keyMessages: How ministers can turn multisectoral coordination from a committee on paper into a system with authority
writeupSummary: AMR is the textbook One Health problem, yet in most African countries multisectoral coordination exists on paper—committees lack legal mandate, budget authority and functioning secretariats. Regional surveys rate coordination the top priority but sustainable investment lowest. The brief draws on the Quadripartite Joint Plan to show how to make governance real.
callToAction: Ministers should institutionalise One Health governance in law, fund permanent secretariats, integrate the environment sector, and report progress to leadership.
alignment: One Health (all sectors) & Action — multisectoral coordination

title: Designing Integrated AMR/AMU Surveillance — GLASS, ATLASS, WOAH and TISSA
keyTakeaway: Ministers should strengthen the laboratory base, harmonise global surveillance frameworks, invest in interoperability, and build antimicrobial-use data systems.
executiveSummary: Effective AMR control depends on surveillance tracking both resistance and antimicrobial use across human, animal and environmental sectors, yet this integrated picture barely exists in Africa—fragmented by sector and concentrated in a few laboratories. The brief shows how to design integrated surveillance aligned with the GLASS, ATLASS, WOAH and InFARM frameworks.
keyMessages: How ministers can build surveillance that produces decision-grade data across human, animal and environmental health
writeupSummary: Effective AMR control depends on surveillance tracking both resistance and antimicrobial use across human, animal and environmental sectors, yet this integrated picture barely exists in Africa—fragmented by sector and concentrated in a few laboratories. The brief shows how to design integrated surveillance aligned with the GLASS, ATLASS, WOAH and InFARM frameworks.
callToAction: Ministers should strengthen the laboratory base, harmonise global surveillance frameworks, invest in interoperability, and build antimicrobial-use data systems.
alignment: Innovation & Action — integrated One Health surveillance

title: Strengthening Laboratory Systems for AMR Data Quality
keyTakeaway: Ministers should invest in a quality-assured sentinel laboratory network, pursue accreditation, secure reagent supply, and expand external quality assessment.
executiveSummary: Every AMR statistic, treatment guideline and stewardship decision rests on a laboratory result, yet Africa's laboratory foundation is dangerously thin—the MAAP analysis found only about 1% of network laboratories perform bacteriology. The brief examines the bottleneck and shows how targeted investment in quality management and accreditation can reinforce it.
keyMessages: Why reliable AMR surveillance, stewardship and policy all rest on functioning, quality-assured laboratories
writeupSummary: Every AMR statistic, treatment guideline and stewardship decision rests on a laboratory result, yet Africa's laboratory foundation is dangerously thin—the MAAP analysis found only about 1% of network laboratories perform bacteriology. The brief examines the bottleneck and shows how targeted investment in quality management and accreditation can reinforce it.
callToAction: Ministers should invest in a quality-assured sentinel laboratory network, pursue accreditation, secure reagent supply, and expand external quality assessment.
alignment: Action — strengthening core laboratory/surveillance capacity (LMIC gap)

title: Stewardship and IPC — the Most Cost-Effective AMR Interventions
shortTitle: The Best Value in the Fight
keyTakeaway: Ministers should mandate and resource facility stewardship and IPC programmes with the staff, salary support and diagnostics they require.
executiveSummary: If AMR financing must be prioritised, antimicrobial stewardship and infection prevention and control are where the first money should go—the most cost-effective interventions available. Point-prevalence surveys show 50–80% of African inpatients on antibiotics, much of it inappropriate. Modelling indicates much of the resistance burden is avertible with existing interventions.
keyMessages: Why antimicrobial stewardship and infection prevention deliver the highest return of any AMR investment
writeupSummary: If AMR financing must be prioritised, antimicrobial stewardship and infection prevention and control are where the first money should go—the most cost-effective interventions available. Point-prevalence surveys show 50–80% of African inpatients on antibiotics, much of it inappropriate. Modelling indicates much of the resistance burden is avertible with existing interventions.
callToAction: Ministers should mandate and resource facility stewardship and IPC programmes with the staff, salary support and diagnostics they require.
alignment: One Health–Human & prevention — most cost-effective action

title: Antimicrobial Resistance in Livestock, Food Safety and Agriculture
keyTakeaway: Ministers should phase out growth-promoting antimicrobials, regulate therapeutic use, invest in alternatives, and strengthen food-safety oversight for sustainable growth.
executiveSummary: Africa faces a protein paradox: soaring demand for animal products drives rising antimicrobial reliance in intensified livestock production, accelerating resistance. An estimated 3,558–4,279 tonnes were used in animals across African countries (2015–2019). Resistant bacteria transmit to humans via food and occupational contact. The brief shows how to capture economic gains safely.
keyMessages: How ministers can harness Africa’s food-system growth without seeding the resistance crisis
writeupSummary: Africa faces a protein paradox: soaring demand for animal products drives rising antimicrobial reliance in intensified livestock production, accelerating resistance. An estimated 3,558–4,279 tonnes were used in animals across African countries (2015–2019). Resistant bacteria transmit to humans via food and occupational contact. The brief shows how to capture economic gains safely.
callToAction: Ministers should phase out growth-promoting antimicrobials, regulate therapeutic use, invest in alternatives, and strengthen food-safety oversight for sustainable growth.

title: Environmental AMR — the Missing Pillar of One Health
keyTakeaway: Ministers should establish wastewater surveillance at sentinel sites, conduct baseline environmental surveys, and integrate environmental data into national surveillance.
executiveSummary: Resistance lives, persists and evolves in the environment—wastewater, rivers, soil and groundwater—continuously re-entering human and animal populations, yet environmental surveillance is the most neglected One Health pillar, absent from most national plans. A 2025 review shows wastewater treatment plants are convergence points where resistance accumulates and disseminates.
keyMessages: Why wastewater, soil and water surveillance are essential to understanding and controlling resistance
writeupSummary: Resistance lives, persists and evolves in the environment—wastewater, rivers, soil and groundwater—continuously re-entering human and animal populations, yet environmental surveillance is the most neglected One Health pillar, absent from most national plans. A 2025 review shows wastewater treatment plants are convergence points where resistance accumulates and disseminates.
callToAction: Ministers should establish wastewater surveillance at sentinel sites, conduct baseline environmental surveys, and integrate environmental data into national surveillance.

title: Digital Transformation for AMR — Real-Time Intelligence Across One Health
keyTakeaway: Ministers should mandate interoperability standards, invest in laboratory and prescribing digitalisation, build integrated dashboards, and apply targeted AI responsibly.
executiveSummary: AMR data in Africa are abundant but fragmented and rarely used in time—lab results in paper registers, surveillance compiled late, use data siloed across ministries. The brief examines how digital transformation—interoperability, real-time dashboards, electronic prescribing and AI—converts latent capacity into decisions, citing Kenya's integrated One Health surveillance system.
keyMessages: How interoperable digital systems and AI can turn fragmented AMR data into decisions that change outcomes
writeupSummary: AMR data in Africa are abundant but fragmented and rarely used in time—lab results in paper registers, surveillance compiled late, use data siloed across ministries. The brief examines how digital transformation—interoperability, real-time dashboards, electronic prescribing and AI—converts latent capacity into decisions, citing Kenya's integrated One Health surveillance system.
callToAction: Ministers should mandate interoperability standards, invest in laboratory and prescribing digitalisation, build integrated dashboards, and apply targeted AI responsibly.
alignment: Innovation — digital tools & AI (named focus; 'success stories')

title: Post-Shock Political–Economic Conditions for AMR in Africa (March 2026)
keyTakeaway: Ministers should protect the irreducible core with domestic financing, mainstream AMR into national budgets, and deepen regional pooling.
executiveSummary: A year after the 2025 financing shock, the brief assesses the post-shock landscape. The abrupt contraction in US development assistance, plus declining bilateral contributions, exposed how much African AMR capacity rested on external grants. A PLOS Medicine analysis documents threats to surveillance, laboratories and One Health programmes across the continent.
keyMessages: How the 2025 funding contraction reshaped the AMR landscape — and what resilience now requires
writeupSummary: A year after the 2025 financing shock, the brief assesses the post-shock landscape. The abrupt contraction in US development assistance, plus declining bilateral contributions, exposed how much African AMR capacity rested on external grants. A PLOS Medicine analysis documents threats to surveillance, laboratories and One Health programmes across the continent.
callToAction: Ministers should protect the irreducible core with domestic financing, mainstream AMR into national budgets, and deepen regional pooling.

title: Ten Calls for Abuja — Key Messages for the 5th Global High-Level Ministerial Conference on AMR
keyTakeaway: African ministers should adopt the ten concrete calls, converting decades of AMR commitment into accountable, financed action at Abuja.
executiveSummary: Abuja will host the 5th Global High-Level Ministerial Conference—the first on African soil—an opportunity to convert prior declarations (Muscat, Jeddah, the UN High-Level Meeting, Davos) into measurable action. This final foundational brief synthesises evidence from the preceding fourteen and distils ten concrete, achievable commitments grounded in technical and political-economy analysis.
keyMessages: A synthesis of evidence and ten concrete commitments to make Abuja a true inflection point
writeupSummary: Abuja will host the 5th Global High-Level Ministerial Conference—the first on African soil—an opportunity to convert prior declarations (Muscat, Jeddah, the UN High-Level Meeting, Davos) into measurable action. This final foundational brief synthesises evidence from the preceding fourteen and distils ten concrete, achievable commitments grounded in technical and political-economy analysis.
callToAction: African ministers should adopt the ten concrete calls, converting decades of AMR commitment into accountable, financed action at Abuja.
alignment: Accountability & Action — synthesis of commitments for Abuja

title: Maternal, Neonatal & Paediatric AMR — Africa's Largest Avoidable Mortality Bucket
shortTitle: The First Breath, the First Fight
keyTakeaway: The Abuja Outcome Document should elevate maternal, neonatal and paediatric AMR to a discrete, costed pillar with measurable 2030 targets.
executiveSummary: Africa carries 56% of global maternal deaths and almost half of neonatal deaths, with sepsis, pneumonia and meningitis leading. AMR—ESBL Klebsiella and E. coli in NICUs, carbapenem-resistant Acinetobacter, drug-resistant Group B Streptococcus—is the largest avoidable component. No other AMR sub-population offers comparable return on investment toward 2030 targets.
keyMessages: Why neonatal sepsis, paediatric Klebsiella, drug-resistant Group B Streptococcus and unsafe maternal infections deserve their own line in every African AMR National Action Plan and the Abuja Outcome Document.
writeupSummary: Africa carries 56% of global maternal deaths and almost half of neonatal deaths, with sepsis, pneumonia and meningitis leading. AMR—ESBL Klebsiella and E. coli in NICUs, carbapenem-resistant Acinetobacter, drug-resistant Group B Streptococcus—is the largest avoidable component. No other AMR sub-population offers comparable return on investment toward 2030 targets.
callToAction: The Abuja Outcome Document should elevate maternal, neonatal and paediatric AMR to a discrete, costed pillar with measurable 2030 targets.
alignment: One Health–Human & Equity — vulnerable clinical populations

title: Drug-Resistant Tuberculosis on the African Continent and the AMR Architecture
keyTakeaway: Abuja should fuse the TB and AMR responses, leveraging mature TB infrastructure to strengthen the broader continental AMR architecture.
executiveSummary: Tuberculosis is Africa's oldest, largest and most under-integrated AMR challenge—the continent carries 23% of global TB burden and six of thirty high-burden MDR-TB countries, with detection near 40%. TB and AMR programmes operate in parallel; TB drug-susceptibility data rarely feeds GLASS. The brief urges fusing the two responses.
keyMessages: Why MDR-TB and XDR-TB are simultaneously Africa's oldest and most under-integrated AMR challenge — and why the Abuja Outcome Document must finally fuse the TB and AMR responses.
writeupSummary: Tuberculosis is Africa's oldest, largest and most under-integrated AMR challenge—the continent carries 23% of global TB burden and six of thirty high-burden MDR-TB countries, with detection near 40%. TB and AMR programmes operate in parallel; TB drug-susceptibility data rarely feeds GLASS. The brief urges fusing the two responses.
callToAction: Abuja should fuse the TB and AMR responses, leveraging mature TB infrastructure to strengthen the broader continental AMR architecture.

title: Drug-Resistant Gonorrhoea and Sexually Transmitted Infections in Africa
keyTakeaway: Abuja should commit countries to WHO EGASP enrolment, tertiary aetiological STI diagnosis, and the overdue syndromic-to-aetiological transition.
executiveSummary: Drug-resistant Neisseria gonorrhoeae appears on every WHO priority list but in almost no African plan. STI care still relies on 1990s syndromic management that ignores the organism and its resistance. Resistance to first-line dual therapy is documented, syphilis is re-emerging, and Mycoplasma genitalium resistance is rising across settings.
keyMessages: Why Neisseria gonorrhoeae, syphilis re-emergence and rising STI resistance demand a continental syndromic-to-aetiological transition in the Abuja Outcome Document.
writeupSummary: Drug-resistant Neisseria gonorrhoeae appears on every WHO priority list but in almost no African plan. STI care still relies on 1990s syndromic management that ignores the organism and its resistance. Resistance to first-line dual therapy is documented, syphilis is re-emerging, and Mycoplasma genitalium resistance is rising across settings.
callToAction: Abuja should commit countries to WHO EGASP enrolment, tertiary aetiological STI diagnosis, and the overdue syndromic-to-aetiological transition.

title: HIV, Malaria, Tuberculosis and AMR — the Forgotten Intersections
keyTakeaway: Abuja should integrate co-trimoxazole resistance surveillance, scale antimalarial resistance monitoring, and reform diagnostic-test adherence across the disease programmes.
executiveSummary: Africa carries the world's heaviest combined HIV, malaria and TB burden—two-thirds of people with HIV, over 90% of malaria, 23% of TB. Long-term co-trimoxazole prophylaxis, empirical antibiotics for malarial fevers, and antimalarial resistance create vast under-recognised selection pressure. The brief argues these intersections need a discrete operational domain.
keyMessages: Why opportunistic infections in immunocompromised African populations and antimalarial drug resistance must enter the Abuja Outcome Document as a discrete operational domain.
writeupSummary: Africa carries the world's heaviest combined HIV, malaria and TB burden—two-thirds of people with HIV, over 90% of malaria, 23% of TB. Long-term co-trimoxazole prophylaxis, empirical antibiotics for malarial fevers, and antimalarial resistance create vast under-recognised selection pressure. The brief argues these intersections need a discrete operational domain.
callToAction: Abuja should integrate co-trimoxazole resistance surveillance, scale antimalarial resistance monitoring, and reform diagnostic-test adherence across the disease programmes.

title: Surgical Antimicrobial Resistance and Surgical Site Infections in Africa
shortTitle: The Operating Theatre and the Microbe
keyTakeaway: Abuja should integrate surgical AMR as a discrete pillar, embedding the WHO surgical safety bundle within national surgical plans.
executiveSummary: Surgical site infections are Africa's most common healthcare-associated infections, affecting roughly 9.6% of surgical patients—three times high-income rates—and increasingly involve multidrug-resistant organisms. As surgical access expands through national surgical plans, the AMR implications have received little attention. Surgical AMR is the largest under-counted hospital burden, and the most tractable.
keyMessages: Why surgical AMR is the largest under-counted AMR burden in African hospitals and why the WHO surgical safety bundle must be embedded as an AMR commitment in the Abuja Outcome Document.
writeupSummary: Surgical site infections are Africa's most common healthcare-associated infections, affecting roughly 9.6% of surgical patients—three times high-income rates—and increasingly involve multidrug-resistant organisms. As surgical access expands through national surgical plans, the AMR implications have received little attention. Surgical AMR is the largest under-counted hospital burden, and the most tractable.
callToAction: Abuja should integrate surgical AMR as a discrete pillar, embedding the WHO surgical safety bundle within national surgical plans.

title: Quality Antibiotic Availability, Reserve Drugs and the African Supply Gap
keyTakeaway: Abuja should increase Access-group availability, make Reserve drugs available at tertiary hospitals, and eliminate falsified products via continental instruments.
executiveSummary: African AMR policy faces a paradox of simultaneous under-access and over-use: roughly 5.7 million deaths yearly stem from lack of access to effective antimicrobials, while inappropriate use drives resistance. Access-group antibiotics often fall below the WHO AWaRe 60% target. Resolving both requires increasing access while protecting reserve drugs.
keyMessages: Why African AMR responses must simultaneously increase access to first-line antibiotics for under-served populations and protect Reserve-group drugs from misuse — and what the Abuja Outcome Document must commit to deliver.
writeupSummary: African AMR policy faces a paradox of simultaneous under-access and over-use: roughly 5.7 million deaths yearly stem from lack of access to effective antimicrobials, while inappropriate use drives resistance. Access-group antibiotics often fall below the WHO AWaRe 60% target. Resolving both requires increasing access while protecting reserve drugs.
callToAction: Abuja should increase Access-group availability, make Reserve drugs available at tertiary hospitals, and eliminate falsified products via continental instruments.
alignment: Access to antimicrobials in LMICs (named focus) & Equity

title: Vaccines as the Most Cost-Effective Antimicrobial Resistance Countermeasure
keyTakeaway: Abuja should commit to 90% coverage of AMR-relevant vaccines, continental typhoid and cholera expansion, and pipeline early-adoption planning.
executiveSummary: Vaccines are the single most cost-effective AMR countermeasure—each averted infection removes the need for antibiotics and the selection pressure they create. WHO estimates universal pneumococcal, typhoid, rotavirus and influenza vaccination would prevent over 510,000 AMR deaths annually, concentrated in sub-Saharan Africa. Coverage remains uneven despite strong immunisation architecture.
keyMessages: Why expanding pneumococcal, typhoid, rotavirus, cholera and (future) Group B Streptococcus and Klebsiella vaccines across Africa is the highest-yield AMR investment available to ministers — and what the Abuja Outcome Document must commit to.
writeupSummary: Vaccines are the single most cost-effective AMR countermeasure—each averted infection removes the need for antibiotics and the selection pressure they create. WHO estimates universal pneumococcal, typhoid, rotavirus and influenza vaccination would prevent over 510,000 AMR deaths annually, concentrated in sub-Saharan Africa. Coverage remains uneven despite strong immunisation architecture.
callToAction: Abuja should commit to 90% coverage of AMR-relevant vaccines, continental typhoid and cholera expansion, and pipeline early-adoption planning.
alignment: Innovation & prevention — vaccines/access (named gap: preventive measures)

title: Diagnostics and Point-of-Care Testing as the Missing Pillar of African AMR Response
shortTitle: The Right Test at the Right Time
keyTakeaway: Abuja should elevate diagnostics as a discrete pillar—CRP at district hospitals, malaria RDT adherence, regional bacteriology, and AMRSNET expansion.
executiveSummary: Diagnostic uncertainty is the largest single driver of inappropriate prescribing—when clinicians cannot distinguish bacterial from viral infection or identify organisms, empirical broad-spectrum prescribing follows. Only 1.3% of sub-Saharan laboratories perform bacteriology. The gap spans point-of-care biomarkers, malaria and TB tests, and bacteriology, all needed as a discrete pillar.
keyMessages: Why CRP, procalcitonin, malaria RDT-confirmed prescribing and bacteriology at point-of-care must enter the Abuja Outcome Document as a discrete pillar.
writeupSummary: Diagnostic uncertainty is the largest single driver of inappropriate prescribing—when clinicians cannot distinguish bacterial from viral infection or identify organisms, empirical broad-spectrum prescribing follows. Only 1.3% of sub-Saharan laboratories perform bacteriology. The gap spans point-of-care biomarkers, malaria and TB tests, and bacteriology, all needed as a discrete pillar.
callToAction: Abuja should elevate diagnostics as a discrete pillar—CRP at district hospitals, malaria RDT adherence, regional bacteriology, and AMRSNET expansion.
alignment: Access to diagnostics in LMICs (named focus) & Innovation

title: Africa's Health Workforce and the AMR Imperative
shortTitle: The Hands That Hold the Future
keyTakeaway: Abuja should commit to AMR-relevant workforce expansion—clinicians, microbiologists, IPC nurses, stewardship pharmacists—as a discrete continental pillar.
executiveSummary: Africa's health workforce is the binding constraint on every AMR pillar—the continent carries 24% of global disease burden but only 3% of the workforce, below the 4.45-per-1,000 threshold. AMR-relevant cadres concentrate in tertiary urban settings; clinical microbiology training exists in fewer than fifteen countries. Workforce shortages compound structural gaps.
keyMessages: Why training, retaining and deploying clinicians, microbiologists, IPC nurses and AMR specialists is the binding constraint on every other pillar of the African AMR response.
writeupSummary: Africa's health workforce is the binding constraint on every AMR pillar—the continent carries 24% of global disease burden but only 3% of the workforce, below the 4.45-per-1,000 threshold. AMR-relevant cadres concentrate in tertiary urban settings; clinical microbiology training exists in fewer than fifteen countries. Workforce shortages compound structural gaps.
callToAction: Abuja should commit to AMR-relevant workforce expansion—clinicians, microbiologists, IPC nurses, stewardship pharmacists—as a discrete continental pillar.

title: Pharmacists, Drug Vendors and the Informal Antibiotic Market in Africa
keyTakeaway: Abuja should formally integrate community pharmacies and informal vendors into continental AMR governance using tested training and regulatory pathways.
executiveSummary: Most antibiotics in Africa are dispensed by community pharmacies, drug shops, patent-medicine vendors and informal sellers, with 70–90% of transactions occurring without prescription, often involving Watch or Reserve agents. This dispensing continuum is the largest consumption channel yet the most under-engaged governance challenge. Tested interventions exist but plans rarely include them.
keyMessages: Why community pharmacy and informal drug-selling cadres are simultaneously the largest antibiotic dispensers and the largest under-engaged AMR partners on the continent.
writeupSummary: Most antibiotics in Africa are dispensed by community pharmacies, drug shops, patent-medicine vendors and informal sellers, with 70–90% of transactions occurring without prescription, often involving Watch or Reserve agents. This dispensing continuum is the largest consumption channel yet the most under-engaged governance challenge. Tested interventions exist but plans rarely include them.
callToAction: Abuja should formally integrate community pharmacies and informal vendors into continental AMR governance using tested training and regulatory pathways.

title: Africa's Veterinary Workforce and the One Health AMR Imperative
keyTakeaway: Abuja should elevate the veterinary and para-veterinary workforce as a discrete One Health AMR pillar of the continental response.
executiveSummary: Africa's veterinary workforce sits at the operational core of One Health AMR, yet the continent has the world's lowest veterinarian-per-livestock ratio. Veterinary schools concentrate in few countries, licensure varies, and animal antimicrobial use is large and largely unmeasured. WOAH evaluations repeatedly identify workforce shortages as the binding constraint on animal-health AMR action.
keyMessages: Why African veterinary workforce shortages and the gap between animal-health and human-health AMR governance are the largest under-addressed One Health challenge — and what the Abuja Outcome Document must commit to fix.
writeupSummary: Africa's veterinary workforce sits at the operational core of One Health AMR, yet the continent has the world's lowest veterinarian-per-livestock ratio. Veterinary schools concentrate in few countries, licensure varies, and animal antimicrobial use is large and largely unmeasured. WOAH evaluations repeatedly identify workforce shortages as the binding constraint on animal-health AMR action.
callToAction: Abuja should elevate the veterinary and para-veterinary workforce as a discrete One Health AMR pillar of the continental response.

title: Antimicrobial Resistance in African Refugee and Internally Displaced Populations
keyTakeaway: Abuja should integrate humanitarian populations into AMR governance with standing coordination, sentinel surveillance, and continental WASH-AMR camp standards.
executiveSummary: Africa hosts about 36 million refugees, asylum-seekers and internally displaced people—the world's highest concentration—yet they are nearly invisible in national AMR plans. Displaced populations face every AMR risk factor: overcrowding, poor WASH, broken referral chains and unmonitored humanitarian prescribing. Camp outbreak investigations consistently document multidrug-resistant infections.
keyMessages: Why the continent's 36 million displaced people are invisible in AMR National Action Plans — and why the Abuja Outcome Document must commit to humanitarian-AMR integration.
writeupSummary: Africa hosts about 36 million refugees, asylum-seekers and internally displaced people—the world's highest concentration—yet they are nearly invisible in national AMR plans. Displaced populations face every AMR risk factor: overcrowding, poor WASH, broken referral chains and unmonitored humanitarian prescribing. Camp outbreak investigations consistently document multidrug-resistant infections.
callToAction: Abuja should integrate humanitarian populations into AMR governance with standing coordination, sentinel surveillance, and continental WASH-AMR camp standards.

title: Antimicrobial Resistance in Conflict and Fragile States in Africa
keyTakeaway: Abuja should explicitly address fragile-state AMR through standing coordination, emergency stockpiles, adapted plan guidance, and sentinel surveillance by 2028.
executiveSummary: Africa hosts the largest concentration of fragile and conflict-affected states—22 listed as fragile. Conflict across the Sahel, Horn, Lake Chad, eastern DRC and Sudan has degraded health systems. These states are the continent's largest AMR data blind spot: surveillance absent, laboratories destroyed, oversight minimal, war-wound infections increasingly multidrug-resistant.
keyMessages: Why fragile states represent the largest blind spot in continental AMR data, the highest concentration of selection pressure, and the most urgent governance challenge for the Abuja Outcome Document.
writeupSummary: Africa hosts the largest concentration of fragile and conflict-affected states—22 listed as fragile. Conflict across the Sahel, Horn, Lake Chad, eastern DRC and Sudan has degraded health systems. These states are the continent's largest AMR data blind spot: surveillance absent, laboratories destroyed, oversight minimal, war-wound infections increasingly multidrug-resistant.
callToAction: Abuja should explicitly address fragile-state AMR through standing coordination, emergency stockpiles, adapted plan guidance, and sentinel surveillance by 2028.

title: Climate Change as an Accelerator of Antimicrobial Resistance
keyTakeaway: Abuja should commit countries to climate-AMR integrated plans, vector-borne AMR surveillance, and leveraging climate-adaptation financing for AMR co-benefits.
executiveSummary: Climate change accelerates AMR through convergent pathways: higher temperatures raise bacterial growth and gene transfer; floods overwhelm WASH; drought concentrates people and livestock; vector ranges expand, increasing antibiotic prescribing. Africa is the region most exposed and least equipped to adapt, with 1.5–2.0°C projected warming by 2050.
keyMessages: Why climate variability, extreme weather, vector range expansion and water stress will accelerate African AMR through 2050 — and why the Outcome Document must integrate climate-AMR planning.
writeupSummary: Climate change accelerates AMR through convergent pathways: higher temperatures raise bacterial growth and gene transfer; floods overwhelm WASH; drought concentrates people and livestock; vector ranges expand, increasing antibiotic prescribing. Africa is the region most exposed and least equipped to adapt, with 1.5–2.0°C projected warming by 2050.
callToAction: Abuja should commit countries to climate-AMR integrated plans, vector-borne AMR surveillance, and leveraging climate-adaptation financing for AMR co-benefits.

title: Gender Dynamics in Antimicrobial Resistance in Africa
keyTakeaway: Abuja should commit to gender-AMR integration, women's governance leadership, sex-disaggregated surveillance, and strengthened reproductive-health AMR pathways.
executiveSummary: Gender shapes AMR exposure, prescribing, outcomes and governance: women dominate the health workforce, make household antibiotic decisions, and bear disproportionate burden from UTIs, maternal and post-abortion sepsis, and drug-resistant gonorrhoea. Yet gender analysis is largely absent from national plans and women are under-represented in AMR governance leadership.
keyMessages: Why African women bear disproportionate AMR burden, drive household antibiotic decisions, dominate the health workforce, and remain under-represented in AMR governance — and why gender-AMR integration is overdue.
writeupSummary: Gender shapes AMR exposure, prescribing, outcomes and governance: women dominate the health workforce, make household antibiotic decisions, and bear disproportionate burden from UTIs, maternal and post-abortion sepsis, and drug-resistant gonorrhoea. Yet gender analysis is largely absent from national plans and women are under-represented in AMR governance leadership.
callToAction: Abuja should commit to gender-AMR integration, women's governance leadership, sex-disaggregated surveillance, and strengthened reproductive-health AMR pathways.

title: Urban–Rural AMR Disparities and the Geography of Equity
keyTakeaway: Abuja should commit to subnational AMR planning, tiered laboratory extension, rural primary-care stewardship, telemedicine support, and continental subnational monitoring.
executiveSummary: African AMR architecture is structurally urban—reference laboratories, tertiary microbiology and reserve-antibiotic capacity, and technical groups all sit in capitals. Yet 50–80% of Africans live rurally, where informal drug markets, weak supply chains, no laboratory access and agricultural antimicrobial use are most intense. Subnational data is essentially absent.
keyMessages: Why African AMR architecture remains structurally concentrated in capital and tertiary urban centres — and why the rural majority must be brought into the response.
writeupSummary: African AMR architecture is structurally urban—reference laboratories, tertiary microbiology and reserve-antibiotic capacity, and technical groups all sit in capitals. Yet 50–80% of Africans live rurally, where informal drug markets, weak supply chains, no laboratory access and agricultural antimicrobial use are most intense. Subnational data is essentially absent.
callToAction: Abuja should commit to subnational AMR planning, tiered laboratory extension, rural primary-care stewardship, telemedicine support, and continental subnational monitoring.

title: Water, Sanitation, Hygiene and the African AMR Response
keyTakeaway: Abuja should formalise WASH-AMR integration as a discrete pillar, target 90% healthcare-facility WASH by 2030, and launch a continental scorecard.
executiveSummary: An infection prevented is an antibiotic course averted, making water and sanitation the most cost-effective AMR intervention available. Yet 411 million Africans lack basic drinking water and 779 million lack basic sanitation, driving preventable infection at population scale. WASH and AMR remain governed in separate ministerial silos.
keyMessages: Why preventing infection through WASH is the single highest-value AMR intervention in low-resource settings — and why WASH and AMR remain in separate ministerial silos across most of Africa.
writeupSummary: An infection prevented is an antibiotic course averted, making water and sanitation the most cost-effective AMR intervention available. Yet 411 million Africans lack basic drinking water and 779 million lack basic sanitation, driving preventable infection at population scale. WASH and AMR remain governed in separate ministerial silos.
callToAction: Abuja should formalise WASH-AMR integration as a discrete pillar, target 90% healthcare-facility WASH by 2030, and launch a continental scorecard.

title: Aquaculture, Antimicrobials and the African One Health Agenda
keyTakeaway: Abuja should act preemptively: continental aquaculture use guidance, surveillance in AMRSNET, biosecurity and vaccination alternatives, and an aquaculture plan pillar.
executiveSummary: Aquaculture is Africa's fastest-growing animal-source food sector—Egypt ranks third globally—and a rapidly growing antimicrobial consumer, using human-critical fluoroquinolones, tetracyclines, sulphonamides and macrolides for prophylaxis, treatment and growth promotion. These enter aquatic environments at scale, selecting for resistance in fish pathogens and surrounding microbial communities.
keyMessages: Why Africa's rapidly expanding aquaculture sector is the next AMR frontier — and why aquaculture must enter the Abuja Outcome Document as a discrete One Health domain.
writeupSummary: Aquaculture is Africa's fastest-growing animal-source food sector—Egypt ranks third globally—and a rapidly growing antimicrobial consumer, using human-critical fluoroquinolones, tetracyclines, sulphonamides and macrolides for prophylaxis, treatment and growth promotion. These enter aquatic environments at scale, selecting for resistance in fish pathogens and surrounding microbial communities.
callToAction: Abuja should act preemptively: continental aquaculture use guidance, surveillance in AMRSNET, biosecurity and vaccination alternatives, and an aquaculture plan pillar.

title: Plant Health, Crop Antimicrobials and African AMR Governance
keyTakeaway: Abuja should elevate plant health as a discrete AMR pillar, addressing azole cross-resistance clinical risk and export market-access risk.
executiveSummary: Plant health is the under-recognised fourth One Health pillar. Crop antimicrobials—azole fungicides, streptomycin and oxytetracycline bactericides—are applied at scale across African agriculture. Azole fungicides on staple and export crops select cross-resistance in Aspergillus fumigatus, a major cause of life-threatening invasive aspergillosis in immunocompromised humans, with growing horticulture export use.
keyMessages: Why fungicide overuse on African crops is a continental AMR risk for human medicine — and why plant health must enter the Abuja Outcome Document as the fourth One Health pillar.
writeupSummary: Plant health is the under-recognised fourth One Health pillar. Crop antimicrobials—azole fungicides, streptomycin and oxytetracycline bactericides—are applied at scale across African agriculture. Azole fungicides on staple and export crops select cross-resistance in Aspergillus fumigatus, a major cause of life-threatening invasive aspergillosis in immunocompromised humans, with growing horticulture export use.
callToAction: Abuja should elevate plant health as a discrete AMR pillar, addressing azole cross-resistance clinical risk and export market-access risk.

title: Pharmaceutical Manufacturing, Effluent and African AMR
keyTakeaway: Abuja should task the African Medicines Agency with continental effluent discharge standards and mandate monitoring capacity at every pharmaceutical regulator.
executiveSummary: Pharmaceutical manufacturing is a recognised AMR vector—active ingredients released into water create localised selection pressure transferable to pathogens. African manufacturing is expanding rapidly under the AU Pharmaceutical Manufacturing Plan and African Medicines Agency. Precedents from Hyderabad and European effluent regulation show this is a tractable, regulator-actionable domain.
keyMessages: Why pharmaceutical effluent from African manufacturing must be regulated as an AMR vector — and why the Abuja Outcome Document is the opportunity to establish continental discharge standards.
writeupSummary: Pharmaceutical manufacturing is a recognised AMR vector—active ingredients released into water create localised selection pressure transferable to pathogens. African manufacturing is expanding rapidly under the AU Pharmaceutical Manufacturing Plan and African Medicines Agency. Precedents from Hyderabad and European effluent regulation show this is a tractable, regulator-actionable domain.
callToAction: Abuja should task the African Medicines Agency with continental effluent discharge standards and mandate monitoring capacity at every pharmaceutical regulator.

title: Traditional Medicine, Antimicrobial Use and African AMR
keyTakeaway: Abuja should engage traditional medicine on its own terms—plan pillar, pharmacopoeial standards, healer partnership, and systematic medicinal-plant research.
executiveSummary: Traditional medicine is the most widely used healthcare in many African countries—WHO estimates up to 80% rely on it. It is simultaneously an AMR risk, through informal antibiotic use, adulterated remedies and delayed referral, and an opportunity, through antimicrobial properties of medicinal plants. Continental traditional-medicine architecture is well-established but under-engaged.
keyMessages: Why African traditional medicine is simultaneously an AMR vector and an under-explored AMR resource — and why the Abuja Outcome Document must finally engage this domain on its own terms.
writeupSummary: Traditional medicine is the most widely used healthcare in many African countries—WHO estimates up to 80% rely on it. It is simultaneously an AMR risk, through informal antibiotic use, adulterated remedies and delayed referral, and an opportunity, through antimicrobial properties of medicinal plants. Continental traditional-medicine architecture is well-established but under-engaged.
callToAction: Abuja should engage traditional medicine on its own terms—plan pillar, pharmacopoeial standards, healer partnership, and systematic medicinal-plant research.

title: Antimicrobial Research and Development on the African Continent
keyTakeaway: Abuja should commit Africa to an active global R&D role—discovery, clinical trials, and regulatory leadership—coordinating its world-class research institutions.
executiveSummary: Africa contributes less than 2% of global antimicrobial R&D investment, under 3% of clinical trials and under 1% of discovery science, while the global pipeline itself is thin. This deficit means novel antimicrobials arrive late and expensive, African pathogen profiles are under-represented, and the continent has limited trial capacity.
keyMessages: Why Africa cannot remain a passive recipient of antimicrobials and must build its own R&D capacity — from clinical trials to discovery science — ahead of the 2030 horizon.
writeupSummary: Africa contributes less than 2% of global antimicrobial R&D investment, under 3% of clinical trials and under 1% of discovery science, while the global pipeline itself is thin. This deficit means novel antimicrobials arrive late and expensive, African pathogen profiles are under-represented, and the continent has limited trial capacity.
callToAction: Abuja should commit Africa to an active global R&D role—discovery, clinical trials, and regulatory leadership—coordinating its world-class research institutions.
alignment: Innovation — new tools and R&D (named conference focus)

title: Local Pharmaceutical Manufacturing of Antimicrobials — From Aspiration to Architecture
keyTakeaway: Abuja should embed continental antimicrobial manufacturing as a discrete pillar within the AU pharmaceutical industrial agenda and countermeasures roadmap.
executiveSummary: Africa imports 70–90% of its pharmaceutical needs and manufactures under 1% of active ingredients; antimicrobial import dependence is even higher. COVID-19 exposed the strategic risk when supply chains seized. The AU Pharmaceutical Manufacturing Plan and Continental Countermeasures Roadmap envision self-sufficiency by 2040 with intermediate 2030 milestones.
keyMessages: Why the African Union Pharmaceutical Manufacturing Plan, COVID-19 lessons and the post-aid future require continental antimicrobial manufacturing capacity as a strategic AMR commitment.
writeupSummary: Africa imports 70–90% of its pharmaceutical needs and manufactures under 1% of active ingredients; antimicrobial import dependence is even higher. COVID-19 exposed the strategic risk when supply chains seized. The AU Pharmaceutical Manufacturing Plan and Continental Countermeasures Roadmap envision self-sufficiency by 2040 with intermediate 2030 milestones.
callToAction: Abuja should embed continental antimicrobial manufacturing as a discrete pillar within the AU pharmaceutical industrial agenda and countermeasures roadmap.

title: The African Medicines Agency and Continental Antimicrobial Regulation
keyTakeaway: Abuja should commit ministerially to the Agency's full operationalisation—universal ratification, sustained financing, and deepened regulatory-harmonisation integration.
executiveSummary: The African Medicines Agency, established by treaty in 2019 and headquartered in Kigali, is Africa's most significant pharmaceutical regulatory reform, harmonising regulation across 54 states. For AMR it is the mechanism through which novel antimicrobials, generics and non-traditional agents reach African markets at scale, underpinning manufacturing and the AfCFTA pharmaceutical annex.
keyMessages: Why AMA, the most important pharmaceutical regulatory reform in African history, must be the backbone of continental AMR action — and what the Abuja Outcome Document should commit to securing it.
writeupSummary: The African Medicines Agency, established by treaty in 2019 and headquartered in Kigali, is Africa's most significant pharmaceutical regulatory reform, harmonising regulation across 54 states. For AMR it is the mechanism through which novel antimicrobials, generics and non-traditional agents reach African markets at scale, underpinning manufacturing and the AfCFTA pharmaceutical annex.
callToAction: Abuja should commit ministerially to the Agency's full operationalisation—universal ratification, sustained financing, and deepened regulatory-harmonisation integration.

title: Substandard and Falsified Antibiotics on the African Market
keyTakeaway: Abuja should commit to MEDICRIME ratification, continental falsified-medicine surveillance through AMRSNET, an INTERPOL enforcement compact, and WHO surveillance participation.
executiveSummary: Substandard and falsified medicines are a major unaddressed AMR driver—WHO estimates one in ten medical products in low- and middle-income countries is affected, with antibiotics common targets. They drive resistance through under-dosing and treatment failure. The continental response is fragmented; the MEDICRIME Convention is ratified by few African states.
keyMessages: Why substandard and falsified antimicrobials remain one of the largest unaddressed AMR drivers on the continent — and why the Abuja Outcome Document must commit to continental enforcement.
writeupSummary: Substandard and falsified medicines are a major unaddressed AMR driver—WHO estimates one in ten medical products in low- and middle-income countries is affected, with antibiotics common targets. They drive resistance through under-dosing and treatment failure. The continental response is fragmented; the MEDICRIME Convention is ratified by few African states.
callToAction: Abuja should commit to MEDICRIME ratification, continental falsified-medicine surveillance through AMRSNET, an INTERPOL enforcement compact, and WHO surveillance participation.

title: Communication, Behaviour Change and Public Engagement for AMR in Africa
keyTakeaway: Abuja should commit to evidence-based, sustained, evaluated AMR communication, leveraging Africa's HIV, TB, malaria and immunisation community-engagement infrastructure.
executiveSummary: AMR is a behavioural phenomenon as much as microbiological—over-prescribing, patient pressure, self-medication, incomplete courses, antibiotic sharing and poor IPC behaviours all drive it. WHO's communication pillar receives the least resourcing; World AMR Awareness Week's impact is weakly evaluated, and behaviour-change campaigns are typically project-based with limited theoretical grounding.
keyMessages: Why behaviour change campaigns, public engagement, and community-level communication must become an evidence-based, well-financed pillar of African AMR action.
writeupSummary: AMR is a behavioural phenomenon as much as microbiological—over-prescribing, patient pressure, self-medication, incomplete courses, antibiotic sharing and poor IPC behaviours all drive it. WHO's communication pillar receives the least resourcing; World AMR Awareness Week's impact is weakly evaluated, and behaviour-change campaigns are typically project-based with limited theoretical grounding.
callToAction: Abuja should commit to evidence-based, sustained, evaluated AMR communication, leveraging Africa's HIV, TB, malaria and immunisation community-engagement infrastructure.
alignment: Action — behaviour change and local engagement

title: Religious and Traditional Leaders as Allies in the African AMR Response
shortTitle: The Pulpit, the Chief and the Microbe
keyTakeaway: Abuja should formalise a continental faith and traditional-leaders AMR compact with national-plan integration and validated partnership models.
executiveSummary: African religious institutions and traditional authorities command community trust and reach no government system can match—faith-based networks deliver 30–40% of sub-Saharan healthcare, and traditional leaders are daily community decision-makers. These networks drove COVID-19, polio, HIV and Ebola successes, yet AMR engagement is essentially absent from plans and frameworks.
keyMessages: Why African faith institutions and traditional authorities — with their unmatched community reach, trust capital and cultural authority — are the most under-leveraged AMR partners on the continent.
writeupSummary: African religious institutions and traditional authorities command community trust and reach no government system can match—faith-based networks deliver 30–40% of sub-Saharan healthcare, and traditional leaders are daily community decision-makers. These networks drove COVID-19, polio, HIV and Ebola successes, yet AMR engagement is essentially absent from plans and frameworks.
callToAction: Abuja should formalise a continental faith and traditional-leaders AMR compact with national-plan integration and validated partnership models.

title: Schools, Universities and Youth Advocacy in the African AMR Response
shortTitle: From the Classroom to the Clinic
keyTakeaway: Abuja should commit to long-horizon AMR investment in schools, universities and youth advocacy, building competencies and a political constituency.
executiveSummary: Africa has the world's youngest population—median age 19, 60% under 25—so the generation that will live AMR's consequences is in school today. Yet AMR education is nearly absent from curricula and youth networks, and few professional training programmes treat it as a discrete competency. The opportunity is correspondingly large.
keyMessages: Why AMR education across primary, secondary and tertiary institutions, plus youth advocacy networks, are the long-horizon investment in continental AMR action.
writeupSummary: Africa has the world's youngest population—median age 19, 60% under 25—so the generation that will live AMR's consequences is in school today. Yet AMR education is nearly absent from curricula and youth networks, and few professional training programmes treat it as a discrete competency. The opportunity is correspondingly large.
callToAction: Abuja should commit to long-horizon AMR investment in schools, universities and youth advocacy, building competencies and a political constituency.
alignment: Action & inclusive leadership — long-horizon local action

title: Media, Journalism and Public Discourse on Antimicrobial Resistance
keyTakeaway: Abuja should commit to a continental AMR media partnership—journalist compacts, fellowships, a story bank, prizes, and civil-society coordination.
executiveSummary: African media coverage of AMR is sparse, episodic and externally driven, originating mostly from press releases or timed to awareness weeks. Health journalism networks are active but small. This matters because political constituencies are built through public discourse—as HIV, climate and COVID-19 demonstrated—and AMR has not yet made that transition.
keyMessages: Why media coverage of AMR in Africa is sparse, episodic and externally driven — and why ministerial commitment to African health journalism is needed to build domestic political constituencies.
writeupSummary: African media coverage of AMR is sparse, episodic and externally driven, originating mostly from press releases or timed to awareness weeks. Health journalism networks are active but small. This matters because political constituencies are built through public discourse—as HIV, climate and COVID-19 demonstrated—and AMR has not yet made that transition.
callToAction: Abuja should commit to a continental AMR media partnership—journalist compacts, fellowships, a story bank, prizes, and civil-society coordination.

title: Trade, Cross-Border Movement and AMR in Africa
keyTakeaway: Abuja should build AMR-sensitivity into trade through sanitary provisions, regional cooperation, informal-trade engagement, and trade-agriculture-health ministerial coordination.
executiveSummary: AMR does not respect borders—resistant pathogens move with people, animals, food and goods. The African Continental Free Trade Area will sharply increase intra-African movement of livestock, food and pharmaceuticals, raising transmission potential. The landscape spans regulated trade and massive informal livestock flows; cross-border veterinary cooperation remains unevenly implemented.
keyMessages: Why the African Continental Free Trade Area, livestock movement corridors and food trade must be central to continental AMR strategy.
writeupSummary: AMR does not respect borders—resistant pathogens move with people, animals, food and goods. The African Continental Free Trade Area will sharply increase intra-African movement of livestock, food and pharmaceuticals, raising transmission potential. The landscape spans regulated trade and massive informal livestock flows; cross-border veterinary cooperation remains unevenly implemented.
callToAction: Abuja should build AMR-sensitivity into trade through sanitary provisions, regional cooperation, informal-trade engagement, and trade-agriculture-health ministerial coordination.

title: Subnational Implementation — The Frontier of African AMR Action
keyTakeaway: Abuja should commit to building the subnational AMR implementation layer—committees, coordinators, financing and data—currently the weakest link.
executiveSummary: Continental commitments are signed by heads of state and plans approved by ministers, but AMR is fought in district hospitals, county laboratories and community drug shops. The subnational layer is where implementation actually happens and where the largest gap sits. Health-system structures vary widely, making subnational capacity highly uneven.
keyMessages: Why district health systems, provincial AMR committees and county-level implementation are the decisive layer between continental commitments and actual change.
writeupSummary: Continental commitments are signed by heads of state and plans approved by ministers, but AMR is fought in district hospitals, county laboratories and community drug shops. The subnational layer is where implementation actually happens and where the largest gap sits. Health-system structures vary widely, making subnational capacity highly uneven.
callToAction: Abuja should commit to building the subnational AMR implementation layer—committees, coordinators, financing and data—currently the weakest link.

title: Legal Frameworks, Regulation and Enforcement for AMR in Africa
shortTitle: Beyond the Law on the Books
keyTakeaway: Abuja should commit to balanced legal-framework and enforcement strengthening that respects access realities and translates commitments into community change.
executiveSummary: Most African countries have laws requiring antibiotic prescriptions and restricting sales to qualified pharmacies, but few enforce them. The gap between law on the books and in practice is a major barrier, driven by thin inspector workforces, fragmented authority, weak penalties, and population dependence on informal supply.
keyMessages: Why having AMR laws is not the same as enforcing them, and what it would take to close Africa's massive AMR enforcement gap.
writeupSummary: Most African countries have laws requiring antibiotic prescriptions and restricting sales to qualified pharmacies, but few enforce them. The gap between law on the books and in practice is a major barrier, driven by thin inspector workforces, fragmented authority, weak penalties, and population dependence on informal supply.
callToAction: Abuja should commit to balanced legal-framework and enforcement strengthening that respects access realities and translates commitments into community change.

title: Civil Society, Accountability and AMR in Africa
keyTakeaway: Abuja should commit to civil society as an equal AMR partner in plan coordination, budget accountability, monitoring and advocacy.
executiveSummary: African civil society defined the continent's most successful health responses—HIV, TB and malaria—through campaigns like South Africa's Treatment Action Campaign that drove accountability, budget transparency and treatment access. AMR civil-society engagement is comparatively embryonic; while networks like ReAct Africa exist, their depth and political effectiveness lag far behind.
keyMessages: Why African civil society has been essential in HIV, TB and malaria responses — and why AMR will not advance without similar civil society mobilisation.
writeupSummary: African civil society defined the continent's most successful health responses—HIV, TB and malaria—through campaigns like South Africa's Treatment Action Campaign that drove accountability, budget transparency and treatment access. AMR civil-society engagement is comparatively embryonic; while networks like ReAct Africa exist, their depth and political effectiveness lag far behind.
callToAction: Abuja should commit to civil society as an equal AMR partner in plan coordination, budget accountability, monitoring and advocacy.

title: Patient, Survivor and Family Voices in African AMR
keyTakeaway: Abuja should commit to bringing patient and survivor voices into African AMR with the seriousness HIV advocacy was built.
executiveSummary: AMR kills specifically—a newborn lost to drug-resistant Klebsiella sepsis, a patient enduring eighteen months of MDR-TB treatment, a young woman losing a leg to post-surgical infection. These voices have been largely absent from African AMR advocacy. The patient-voice transformation that reshaped HIV policy has not yet reached AMR.
keyMessages: Why patient and AMR survivor voices have been largely absent from African AMR advocacy — and why their inclusion will transform the field.
writeupSummary: AMR kills specifically—a newborn lost to drug-resistant Klebsiella sepsis, a patient enduring eighteen months of MDR-TB treatment, a young woman losing a leg to post-surgical infection. These voices have been largely absent from African AMR advocacy. The patient-voice transformation that reshaped HIV policy has not yet reached AMR.
callToAction: Abuja should commit to bringing patient and survivor voices into African AMR with the seriousness HIV advocacy was built.

title: African South-South Cooperation, Twinning and Continental Learning for AMR
keyTakeaway: Abuja should commit to building South-South cooperation, Africa-to-Africa twinning, and continental learning infrastructure for sustainable, owned AMR action.
executiveSummary: African AMR capacity has been substantially shaped by North-South aid relationships that built genuine capability, but the next decade will be built through Africa-to-Africa learning, twinning and South-South cooperation. Precedents are substantial: Cuban medical brigades, Brazilian HIV cooperation, Indian generics, and growing intra-African medical collaboration.
keyMessages: Why African AMR action will not be built primarily through North-South aid pipelines, but through Africa-to-Africa learning, twinning and South-South cooperation.
writeupSummary: African AMR capacity has been substantially shaped by North-South aid relationships that built genuine capability, but the next decade will be built through Africa-to-Africa learning, twinning and South-South cooperation. Precedents are substantial: Cuban medical brigades, Brazilian HIV cooperation, Indian generics, and growing intra-African medical collaboration.
callToAction: Abuja should commit to building South-South cooperation, Africa-to-Africa twinning, and continental learning infrastructure for sustainable, owned AMR action.

title: AMR in African Island States — Mauritius, Seychelles, Comoros, Madagascar, Cape Verde and São Tomé and Príncipe
keyTakeaway: Abuja should address island-state AMR through inclusion, pooled procurement, and shared climate-AMR adaptation learning as a continental ownership requirement.
executiveSummary: Africa's six island states—Madagascar, Comoros, Seychelles, Mauritius, Cape Verde, and São Tomé and Príncipe, plus Zanzibar—face distinctive AMR challenges mainland-focused action has overlooked: small populations limiting market and laboratory viability, geographic isolation creating import dependence, and the world's highest climate vulnerability. The final brief argues continental architecture must address them.
keyMessages: Why African island states face distinctive AMR challenges — small populations, isolated health systems, high climate vulnerability — and what continental cooperation owes them.
writeupSummary: Africa's six island states—Madagascar, Comoros, Seychelles, Mauritius, Cape Verde, and São Tomé and Príncipe, plus Zanzibar—face distinctive AMR challenges mainland-focused action has overlooked: small populations limiting market and laboratory viability, geographic isolation creating import dependence, and the world's highest climate vulnerability. The final brief argues continental architecture must address them.
callToAction: Abuja should address island-state AMR through inclusion, pooled procurement, and shared climate-AMR adaptation learning as a continental ownership requirement.

title: The Troika, Accountability and Tracking AMR Commitments from Jeddah to Abuja and Beyond
keyTakeaway: Ministers should adopt a concise indicator set, mandate public Troika progress reporting, sustain the financing convening, and link to Africa CDC monitoring.
executiveSummary: A decade of AMR ministerial conferences has produced declarations but little tracking of delivery. The Jeddah Commitments created the Troika—former, present and future host presidencies with Quadripartite support—to maintain momentum and accountability between conferences. Nigeria now chairs it. The brief argues Abuja's value depends on hardening the Troika into a credible, financed accountability engine.
keyMessages: Why a credible accountability mechanism — not another declaration — is what will make Abuja matter, and what African ministers should commit to in the Troika process.
writeupSummary: A decade of AMR ministerial conferences has produced declarations but little tracking of delivery. The Jeddah Commitments created the Troika—former, present and future host presidencies with Quadripartite support—to maintain momentum and accountability between conferences. Nigeria now chairs it. The brief argues Abuja's value depends on hardening the Troika into a credible, financed accountability engine.
callToAction: Ministers should adopt a concise indicator set, mandate public Troika progress reporting, sustain the financing convening, and link to Africa CDC monitoring.
alignment: Accountability & progress review — directly operationalises the Troika (named conference mechanism)

title: The Revised Global Action Plan on AMR and Africa's Stake in the Next Decade
keyTakeaway: Ministers should agree continental positions, push LMIC-calibrated access and equity targets, and re-cost and re-align national action plans to the revised GAP.
executiveSummary: The 2015 Global Action Plan on AMR is being revised by the Quadripartite during 2025–2026 at the 2024 UNGA HLM's request, aligned to a 2030 target of cutting bacterial-AMR deaths 10%. Its adoption window brackets the Abuja conference, giving African ministers a rare moment to embed LMIC financing, access and four-sector One Health realities.
keyMessages: Why the 2026 revision of the Global Action Plan on AMR is a once-in-a-decade chance to embed African and LMIC realities — and what ministers should demand of it.
writeupSummary: The 2015 Global Action Plan on AMR is being revised by the Quadripartite during 2025–2026 at the 2024 UNGA HLM's request, aligned to a 2030 target of cutting bacterial-AMR deaths 10%. Its adoption window brackets the Abuja conference, giving African ministers a rare moment to embed LMIC financing, access and four-sector One Health realities.
callToAction: Ministers should agree continental positions, push LMIC-calibrated access and equity targets, and re-cost and re-align national action plans to the revised GAP.
alignment: Global policy framework — aligns NAPs to the revised GAP-AMR (named conference backdrop)

title: The Independent Panel on Evidence for Action against AMR and African Scientific Leadership
keyTakeaway: Ministers should secure African representation in IPEA, submit continental priority evidence questions, fund research capacity, and translate outputs into national plans.
executiveSummary: The 2024 UNGA declaration mandated the Quadripartite to establish IPEA, a multisectoral science-policy panel (likened to the IPCC) launched December 2025 to generate and translate AMR evidence. Africa produces under 2% of global AMR R&D yet bears the highest burden, risking becoming a passive consumer. The brief argues for African scientific leadership within IPEA.
keyMessages: Why the new global science–policy panel on AMR must reflect African evidence and researchers — and how ministers can ensure the continent shapes, rather than merely consumes, the evidence.
writeupSummary: The 2024 UNGA declaration mandated the Quadripartite to establish IPEA, a multisectoral science-policy panel (likened to the IPCC) launched December 2025 to generate and translate AMR evidence. Africa produces under 2% of global AMR R&D yet bears the highest burden, risking becoming a passive consumer. The brief argues for African scientific leadership within IPEA.
callToAction: Ministers should secure African representation in IPEA, submit continental priority evidence questions, fund research capacity, and translate outputs into national plans.
alignment: Innovation & evidence — African leadership in the new IPEA science-policy panel (named conference backdrop)
